SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear technical claims and supports them with concrete algorithmic definitions (activation-based maps in eq.2 and gradient-based transfer in eqs.3-5) and empirical results across CIFAR, CUB, Scenes and ImageNet. The mathematical expressions are reasonable and implementable, though derivations for the gradient-based transfer require second-order terms and the paper acknowledges practical difficulties (e.g. batch-norm in second backprop), which are not fully resolved. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines (no-transfer, KD, FitNets, l2 regularizer) but some experiments use single runs and lack statistical significance reporting and thorough hyperparameter searches, which weakens confidence in effect sizes. The paper also relies on an empirical assumption (absolute activations indicate importance) that is plausible but heuristic; overall the results justify the main conclusions modulo these experimental limitations.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The idea of explicitly transferring spatial attention maps from teacher to student (both activation- and gradient-based) is a novel, well-motivated perspective on knowledge transfer that is distinct from prior work such as FitNets (hint regression) and standard KD. The work combines visualization ideas (activation statistics, gradient attention) with distillation in a non-trivial way and demonstrates applicability to large-scale ImageNet finetuning, which the authors claim is not common in prior transfer methods. While components (activation statistics, gradient sensitivities, normalization, double backprop) are not individually new, their combination into attention transfer and the experimental demonstrations provide notable novelty beyond prior techniques.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized in the standard and logical format (introduction, related work, methods, experiments, conclusion) and states contributions up front; key equations and attention mapping functions are explicitly defined. However, the manuscript contains duplicated and noisy text in the appendix, some implementation/hyperparameter details are abbreviated or heuristic (e.g. β scheduling and normalization choice), and certain practical aspects (treatment of batch-norm in second backprop, number of runs) are under-specified, which hampers full reproducibility. Figures and tables are referenced to support claims but some were not included in the provided text, making interpretation harder.
